name: a murkiness in bangkok
content: It’s never a particularly happy day here. I stand across a man White-clad and we share an argument—a debate, even. He holds up tall a pair of two: a couple of electronic cigarettes, one Red and the other Green. Red is charged to ninety-seven, she’s got about twenty-something percent of juice left. Green is dead but I carry her for the sake of memory still. I cannot divorce her soul yet; we are married above heavens and beyond mortality. Plus, Red needs a woman to mourn: She is not good.

Mister White gives zero fucks, though. This is an egregious violation of the country’s sovereignty and must be injunctioned at once. I gaze longingly at the e-cigarettes before White flicks his wrist and Red is gone. Green’s dried lashes do not miss Red yet she meets her in hell anyway. White is content. I truly have no reaction to any of this; I am quite indifferent to adversity. I expect the immediate, the spontaneous, the disastrous. One could say that my being here, in fact, is quite immediate, quite spontaneous—you get the idea. Just don’t tell my father.

A second bag arrives. A second security officer scoffs at me. I have not many more seconds left to get out of here. Their hands together pick carefully apart the two zipline chains that close the backpack. They look like they want to look amused; oddly, they really are not quite amused. The second man’s right index peers into my shit. He presents to me a box of ten mini-bottles of hard liquor. It’s got Fireball, Jägermeister, Absolut, you name it. None of them have breathed the air of Delhi, only that of Dubai and then of my best friend’s bedroom and now of this sleazy son of a bitch. He throws them all away. Fuck this guy. I begrudgingly make my way to the bar.
Four drinks down. It is probably time to board by now. I should go. Then, again, maybe I am already gone.

I am sitting outside the smoking area, not inside. I am sitting on a metallic bench next to the shop where they sell cigarettes in boxes and not ones; they only take cash, which I find unsettling, but I have bigger things to worry about right now. There is a Yellow guy frantically searching for something yet I cannot tell what. He seems frustrated and I want to help him; I give him that kind of a look and he smiles at me and comes close.

Yellow is a very simple guy: he wants twenty cigarettes, nine beers, and maybe a bottle of water if I have any money left after that. I give him enough money for water because my father said that was the right thing to do. You know, I don’t have much money either. My best friends sent me the money to book this flight an hour ago when I was standing outside the airport with nothing but tears in my eyes and my dick in my hand. Yellow needed that water more than I needed that money, though. He is a sort of frazzled guy and I sincerely hope his girlfriend calls him back.

He is looking better now. I am at peace with my God. I am really not at peace with my best friends because I have no idea how I will pay them back. I am running away from home at the last second. I am fucking it all up. I am breaking my mother’s heart and I am doing so with knowledge and conscience. My mother will be proud of me when I am dead. I will make her proud very soon. You will see me make her proud. The Yellow guy has left by now. I cry from time to time but smoking a cigarette makes it go away in a couple of minutes. I don’t know why I cry at airports and in airplanes. I think it’s the alcohol, but then again, I could be wrong.

Once again I become the bitchboy. I am crying inside an airplane. I have taken my antidepressants: Fluoxetine, Bupropion, Budweiser. The flight attendant sees me crying. She gives me the warmest hug I have been given in a hot second. I cry even more. She is confused by this, but that is okay; after all, I—no, my friends—have paid quite a lot for this one-way. It’s not a long way, not so far, but you don’t fucking know where I am and that is the point. Sorry. Maybe you pretend not to know where I am. That will be okay also.

She sits next to me and holds up a baby bottle with beer inside. I am dreaming. She gives me a note, and so does her coworker who she is probably training by the way things seem between them; the notes are penned by hand. I smile back because I do not have a clue of what else I might do. I settle back into 1A beneath my fifty kilograms of carry-on baggage. I have a lot of baggage, as you can probably tell by now. I’d like to share some of it with you because you enjoy lifting other people’s shit. I can tell.

This time it feels quite real. I have arrived as I always do; this time, though, nobody knows that I have arrived. I walk off the plane and into the terminal but there is no buggy there to pick me up and nobody with a big sign with my name printed neatly in bold serif. There is also no ATM here and immigration only takes cash. One could say that I am fucked.
I plead with the immigration officer before the walkalator and beg. He lets me through after turmoil to my throat, but this is how we do it in Bangkok. We are in Thailand, motherfuckers.

I don’t know how many beers I’ve had. In fact, “I don’t know” is quite a favourite phrase of mine, and so are “quite” and “in fact” and ones where I place adjectives after nouns to sound like I’m someone who could seduce your mother’s illicit girlfriend. You and I both know that she has an illicit girlfriend. The weather on my flight was quite turbulent if you couldn’t tell by now. “Quite”. There it is.

I am in the taxi and the sun already sets. This is quite the disappointment, you don’t think? All after, one sunset ago I set off for my best friend’s house, one sunrise for Bangkok, and this sunset for my hotel. That is an anticlimax; I should hope to at least make it to the Moon. I disappoint myself often, and the funny thing is that I haven’t been to the bathroom once. My eyes are burning and my nose is on fire. My stomach has been stabbed for the fourth time. I am fucked because I can not take care of my self.

I am writing about people that I found interesting in Bangkok. I am quite selfish, because I have chosen to ignore myself all this time starting October 27th. I will down a fishbowl of vodka and be okay.

As I say this to you, I am in an airplane that hopes to hop to Delhi. The airplane is shaking and I am very very scared. I do not want to die but I find some weird fucking thrill in this shaking and vigorous bouncing from side to side, I find some heartdrops in this sudden whooooooooooop of the rollercoaster. Yeow.

I have never before been East of the River in Bangkok. Emma sent me a web address and I followed it in real life; I feel quite stupid for doing so because Emma is obviously not here and yet I have spent a nonzero amount of money on a cute tote. I still have the tote with me, in case you are curious. Tote is Mister Blue. Blue carries my notebook and pen and laptop and whatever else I command of her at any given time. Blue must be taken care of in Bangkok. Remember that.

I am in a night market in Bangkok and I am walking up circular spiralling stairs. I am quite tired and Blue is heavier and harder to leave than she said she was. I have told Blue that she is a genius and she looks younger than she says she is. She is a bag and she cannot speak, so I should probably stop talking to her. I feel fucking insane. As I write, I talk similarly to two air hostesses: I tell them after I take a leak, they are the most heroic heroes in the sky. My two air hostesses are nurses and chefs and guards and whatnot; my air hostesses could put a buttplug in Osama’s ass and push it forth and back until he submits and agrees to stop bombing American cities. My two air hostesses are the most powerful and talented two people to me. Back to Emma, now. My air hostesses will get their own chapter. I am mentally ill.

Emma. What the fuck? Where are you? You are somebody who is mysterious and I do not find you. I came to Chinatown several times and I stood outside a shop that we agreed upon and yet I do not find you. What the fuck? Is this one you? This silver hair, eyes that say that they want to become a singer despite their having earned a full scholarship to an Ivy League university? This is you, is it not? Emma, Emma, Emma, you are not real. Father, Mother, there is no Emma. Emma is a lie. Emma is the love and cherishing I hold in my heart; so is every taxi driver and security prick and air hostess. I may be a product of capitalism and I may be an enslaver. I will learn to hug my slaves and smell struggles from their armpits and ballsacks. I love my slaves like my mother’s youngest brother loves his dog. I pick my slaves up and kiss them every time I see them. I buy them drinks—I am a lover, God says.

I met Emma at the night market. Well, if I’m being honest, this is a little bit dishonest. I took a taxi to wherever the fuck this place was and I went inside and sat in a restaurant that looked like an airplane. Emma said she would meet me on the airplane. I was on an airplane and so I had seven or so beers (more, probably). I am not a drunkard; I am a calm human being when I drink too much and my depression and anxiety kill themselves.

Emma and I sang loads of songs. I am a good singer. Our songs range from Taylor Swift to Alexander Hamilton; I think that says everything I need to say. The plane shook for two seconds and them calmed back down. I am not scared.
This may be a kind of art piece. I think all good art is born of love and yet I find myself loveless; the best art is born of love and I am a lover. I love you.

Is this okay? Can I give you a little kiss? The air hostess is controlling me. This seatbelt is uncomfortably tight on my chest. I cannot breathe. Please save me because I am in love with you; do not save me for another reason.

Emma is okay. We sang together. I took a picture of a family—a mother, two daughters—with Emma and the musicians. All three of them gave me a hug and I cried for two hours in one bathroom after zero minutes. Emma said she was not feeling so good so I got her food and medicine and enough marijuana to kill an elephant. Every night in Bangkok, I buy enough marijuana to kill an elephant. The tusk hunters fucking love me and so does the crooked woman who has been appointed the finance ministress. She is not good at my language. She still takes my money for some reason and says that she will use it well. I can use my own money however the fuck I want but that is not okay in her books. I will shut up and have my beer. Bangkok is great. In Bangkok, for example, they have free trolley carts in the airport and you can drive them around without pushing the push bar. We have free trolly carts in BKK and WAW but not in EWR or in DEL. This is ridiculous and you know it. What the fuck?

Before I begin, I believe it imperative to say that I love my family. I love my father and mother and brother and I love anyone that is related to me. I love you all. My younger brothers, I love you all even despite the fact that you are eighty years old and fucking rotting away. I love you motherfuckers even after my death; I would love you from the heights of heaven and you know it. You see me in the bathroom in the front; it is closest to 1A.

It is afternoon. The wheels touch ground at last; I am in Bangkok. I left Delhi hazy. A lot of people don’t know that I’ve left. They will know soon. I blinked; I have a Valentine. Mxmtoon loves me, so does Laufey. I am not alone because I fell in October. My parents want me to live—they wish I’d lived that night. I have had six drinks on a flight that was only supposed to serve me three.

I meet the man who immigrates us. I am at the airport and I have smiled for miles millions. The man is always in a mood that you’d describe as rather fatherly; I’m not too sure what that means exactly but I do know that it is true. His voice, the way he leans against his cuboidicle when he’s got no immigrants to immigrate, the way he looks at me when I tell him the story of the deaf and blind dog from Bangkok that I wanted to adopt but couldn’t because I couldn’t, it is all fatherly. He tells me very instructively that we all must work at noname companies and purposeless jobs and slave and slave and circle back and use core competencies to solve burning problems for the CXO of some bumfuck tech company out in Missouri with maybe five dollars (US) of capital in total and half an employee; the CXO is two-timing. Interestingly, my CXO two-times not with a company but with a woman. This is not relevant to you.

I thank the man who immigrates us. He is not done yet. He gives me a big tight hug. Whoever told him that I am a strong man lied. I am a weakling. I melt in his warmth. I have not been hugged by a fatherly figure in a while. I can say that that is okay but you won’t buy my bullshit. That is okay, too.
